findings.description: pickle.loads() deserializes attacker-controllable bytes from Redis (L2), enabling remote code execution via a crafted pickle stream whenever an attacker can write to the cache (exposed/default-auth Redis, shared/multi-tenant Redis, cache poisoning, or attacker-influenced values written via set/warm) — CWE-502 / OWASP A08:2025; fix: replace pickle with JSON or a safe, schema-validated codec.

pickle.dumps() on the write path fixes the on-the-wire format as pickle, which is what forces the unsafe pickle.loads at line 60; the remediation (switch to JSON/safe serialization) must change both the serialize and deserialize sides, so this line is in scope for the fix even though it is not independently exploitable.

_is_expired logic is inverted (`age < self.ttl` returns expired=True for FRESH entries, expired=False for OLD ones): fresh L1 hits are wrongly evicted and re-fetched through the dangerous pickle.loads path (amplifying the deserialization exposure), while stale entries are served indefinitely as fresh — security-relevant when cached values gate auth/session/feature-flag decisions; should be `age > self.ttl`. Full triage deferred to the logic critic.

The local L1 dict (self._local) has no size/entry cap or LRU eviction — combined with the broken expiry (line 47) nothing is reliably evicted, so an attacker driving cache misses with many distinct keys (e.g., via get_or_compute on distinct query params) grows the in-process dict until OOM — unbounded resource allocation DoS, CWE-770; add a max-size bound with eviction.

warm() ingests an arbitrary-size caller-supplied dict and writes every entry with no size limit, providing a second unbounded-allocation vector into both the local dict and Redis (compounds the line-68 DoS); cap or validate the batch size.

RedisClient.setex stores the value but silently drops ttl_seconds, so L2 entries never expire — poisoned/stale values would persist in Redis indefinitely; flagged at low confidence because this is an elided network stub (docstring 'network elided') and real Redis would honor SETEX, but worth noting since the in-file contract never applies a TTL to L2.
